options: Failure of delivery due to weak uterine contractions
Failure of delivery of the fetus in spite of good uterine contractions due to mechanical obstruction
s2.explain: Key: 'in spite of good uterine contractions' — obstructed labor is mechanical, not due to inefficient contractions (uterine inertia).

s2.q: Why is it critical to distinguish obstructed labor from prolonged labor due to uterine inertia?
options: Both are treated the same way
In obstructed labor, more oxytocin will not help and may rupture the uterus
Obstructed labor only occurs in primigravidas
Prolonged labor always requires cesarean section
s2.explain: In obstructed labor, more oxytocin will not help and may cause uterine rupture. The definitive treatment is cesarean section.

s2.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s2.q: The incidence of this condition is highest in which population?
options: Depends on the specific condition and risk factors
s2.explain: Incidence varies by condition — each has its own epidemiology and risk profile.

s3.q: Which of the following is a maternal cause of obstructed labor?
s3.explain: Maternal (passages) causes: contracted pelvis, abnormal pelvis, pelvic tumor, cervical stenosis, vaginal septum. The others are fetal (passenger) causes.

s3.q: Which of the following is a fetal cause of obstructed labor?
s3.explain: Fetal causes: macrosomia, malpresentation, malposition, malformed fetus (hydrocephalos, conjoint twins, fetal ascitis), locked twins.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s3.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s3.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s3.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s4.q: The cardinal vaginal sign of obstructed labor is:
s4.explain: Large caput + moulding = the cardinal vaginal sign. It tells you the head has been trying to descend but the pelvis is too small.

s4.q: Which of the following is an abdominal examination finding in obstructed labor?
options: Fetal heart sounds are always normal
s4.explain: Abdominal findings: uterus hard and tender, pathological retraction ring, distended bladder, fetal distress or absent FHS.

s4.q: According to the source, the first diagnostic criterion for obstructed labor is labor lasting more than:
s4.explain: The source's first diagnostic criterion: labor lasting more than 12 hours — an operationalization of the WHO partogram's action line.

s4.q: The gold standard diagnostic test is:
s4.explain: The best diagnostic method varies by condition — some rely on imaging, others on lab tests or clinical findings.

s4.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s4.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s5.q: The MOST effective single tool for prevention of obstructed labor is:
s5.explain: The partogram is the single most effective bedside tool for early detection of prolonged labor and prevention of obstructed labor.

s5.q: General management of obstructed labor includes all EXCEPT:
options: Correction of dehydration with IV fluids
s5.explain: Oxytocin augmentation is contraindicated in obstructed labor (may rupture uterus). General management: IV fluids, antibiotics, assess the mother.

s5.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s5.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s5.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s5.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s6.q: A maternal complication of obstructed labor is:
s6.explain: Maternal complications: uterine rupture, postpartum hemorrhage, puerperal sepsis, vesicovaginal fistula, maternal death.

s6.q: A fetal complication of obstructed labor is:
s6.explain: Fetal complications: asphyxia, intracranial hemorrhage, fractures, nerve palsies, and neonatal death.

s6.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s6.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.
Fetal well-being is closely linked to maternal condition — multiple fetal complications are possible.

options: All of the above are possible
s6.explain: Obstetric conditions can lead to hemorrhage, infection, thromboembolism, and other complications.
